title: Groundhog Day, 1993 - ★★★★★
reviewText:
[recognizes Phil and runs up to him on the street]
Ned: Phil? Hey, Phil? Phil! Phil Connors? Phil Connors, I thought that was you!
[thinking Ned's just a watcher of the local news]
Phil: Hi, how you doing? Thanks for watching.
[Starts to walk away]

Ned: Hey, hey! Now, don't you tell me you don't remember me because I sure as heckfire remember you.
[bemused]
Phil: Not a chance.
Ned: Ned...
[takes off his hat revealing his completely bald head]

Ned: Ryerson. "Needlenose Ned"? "Ned the Head"? C'mon, buddy. Case Western High. Ned Ryerson: I did the whistling belly-button trick at the high school talent show? Bing! Ned Ryerson: got the shingles real bad senior year, almost didn't graduate? Bing, again. Ned Ryerson: I dated your sister Mary Pat a couple times until you told me not to anymore? Well?
[not even a smidgen of recognition]
Phil: Ned Ryerson?
Ned: Bing!
Phil: Bing. So did you turn pro with that bellybutton thing, Ned?
Ned: No, Phil, I sell insurance.
[deadpan]
Phil: What a shock.
Ned: Do you have life insurance? Because if you do you could always use a little more. Am I right or am I right or am I right? Right? Right?
[feigning politeness and failing]
Phil: Ned, I'd love to stand here and talk with you... but I'm not going to. See you.
[laughs]
Ned: That's okay! I'll walk with you!
[does so, to Phil's annoyance]

Ned: See, whenever I see an opportunity, I charge it like a bull. "Ned the Bull", that's me now. You know, I have friends who live and die by the actuarial tables and I say, "Hey! It's all one big crap-shoot anywho!" Tell me, have you ever heard of single premium life because I think that could really be the ticket for you.
[stops Phil in admiration]

Ned: Oh, God! It is so good to see you! Tell me, what are you doing for dinner?
Phil: Something else. It's been great seeing you, "Needlehead". Take care.
[Phil steps off the curb into a deep, watery puddle. Ned laughs obnoxiously]

Ned: Whoa-ho-ho! Watch out for that first step! It's a doozy!
[as Ned continues to cackle, Phil stomps away]

title: Wicked: For Good, 2025 - ★★★½
reviewText:
why didn't anyone tell me these films are about fascism?

Glinda's entire arc is just: stay silent to stay safe → benefit from the system → realize you're complicit. Glinda embodies "well-meaning liberal who enables fascism" so well.

Ariana Grande deserves everything.

really surprised that i enjoyed these movies, though maybe i shouldn't be since i loved The Wizard of Oz growing up.

in fact, my 3.5 star rating is for the musical performances, which stand on their own pretty well.

the visual language of this film is so... bored. it's not interested in saying anything and the camera just stands like there a passive observer. i knew there was reason to be worried after they sucked all the color out of the set with whatever grading they did — almost like they were trying to broadcast they were sucking the life out of this adaptation.

despite that, the story is told.

title: Midsommar, 2019 - ★★★★★
reviewText:
i think why this captivated me so wholly after my first viewing is because this is Ari Aster channeling a folk storyteller and it's my favorite version of Aster's writing.

his directing is excellent, though I admit he must be forced to cut-cut-cut in editing to tighten things up into a cohesive narrative. the director's cut extends some scenes, but the new scenes were entirely justified in being removed from the final cut.

the theatrical cut is so effective at its 2h23m runtime -- this is the sweet spot. and the ending shocked me almost as much as the reactions I witnessed in the theater after the movie that first, fateful watch. i think i've talked about this on the jxn film club podcast before.

anyway, the national folk festival is coming to jackson. i doubt there will be much folk horror there, but you can bet i'll be trying to talk about it (if it comes up organically); if not regarding Midsommar, it will be very easy for me to talk about Sinners -- which is definitely a Mississippi folk horror story.

some of the character work is a chore but it is perhaps a choice of style over substance; specifically, it's always been my perspective that Justine was written to be annoying but now i'm wondering if she is annoying because the person who was writing her is annoying and I don't know how to determine the difference and whether that even matters

it still made me laugh out loud, it still captured my attention, it still fucking slaps

also put down your phones! put them in another room when you watch a movie and actually let yourself be bored while you watch something for once. i promise, you will survive

title: The Whistler
reviewText:
I won't give this a star rating because this was a test screening, but I have to log this to memorialize watching an English-language film set in a Spanish-speaking country made by Veneszuelan filmmakers who attended the University of Southern Mississippi

even though this ruins my current trend of watching movies from each release year (this takes the place of lifetime entry number 1953) it's worth it because I got to share this experience with my friend Eddie

coming up next for a first-time watch to get me back on track with my release year selections: Seven Samurai (1954)

reviewText:

- a too-early appearance by Lord Summerisle to present a young man for Willow
- an additional sequence of a song that was still included in the theatrical cut

this was a fun spooky season watch. Morgan cooked a venison stew with Welsh rarebit. I had Boddington's pub ale for libation.

title: Sunshine, 2007 - ★★★★½ (contains spoilers)
reviewText:
This review may contain spoilers.

i fell in love with this movie at the viewing of mercury. watching celestial events is a human activity that spans all boundaries and biases.

this movie broke me at Kaneda's death, subsumed by the sun, hearing a final question, "What do you see?" the weight of that moment and the time allowed for it.... this is filmmaking as storytelling. 

where it absolutely hooked me was the boarding of Icarus I and the slow build-up to the terrifying affirmation: "Five crew members." the horror bend here loses people, but it really worked for me the first time and i've come to embrace it. 

the characters are well-written, and the cinematography makes me eyeballs feel good.

watched with lolo and gibby.

title: The Brutalist, 2024 - ★★★★
reviewText:
OVERTURE

Chains and screams and joy and hugs as the Statue of Liberty enters the frame unexpectedly from the top, staring down at us like a giant mother.

THE ENIGMA OF ARRIVAL

Attila welcomes his cousin with tears and introduces him to his quarters as a servant, mentioning only the employee restroom. Attila's insecurity drives László out. The weakness of the man and the woman's suggestion create a reality that is actively hostile toward László.

And this is how America seems to be designed: to separate, to subjugate, and to sell.

Bicycle. Tricycle. Family.

The act of immigration is an act of faith that life can be made, that we can choose better things for ourselves and give more to our friends and family. The promise of America is that all of this is possible.

That promise will seem like a lie by the end of this film, but the hope in this story is not rooted in the American dream."Is there a better description of a cube than that of its construction?"Doylestown represents small-town America, contrasting the NYC arrival with a rural community. The project is only welcomed by the community once it is demonstrated to be sufficiently Christian—a thread that is important in history because of the bloodshed it has caused.

INTERMISSION

I am pleased that the intermission exists in streaming, even if only for a minute. I did enjoy the music during the theatrical intermission.

THE HARD CORE OF BEAUTY

Only Maggie cares enough to show up with László to greet Zsófia and Erzsébet. I think this film shows women honestly, and we are meant to draw conclusions from how loud the men end up being. Perhaps my main criticism of this film is that we spend so much time with these men when I prefer the women, yet it reflects reality to spend more time listening to flawed men.

Beauty's core is hard because it comes from truth.

EPILOGUE

Truth told through storytelling is an expression of the storyteller.

The storyteller all along is Zsófia, and she succumbs to the same ill fate as her uncle László and survives her aunt Erzsébet to speak for him as the concluding point of this film. Harry rapes Zsófia, but it happens off-screen. Harrison rapes László.

The Brutalist depicts this American story in America, but the rape depicted on screen happens in Italy. What are we to make of the rape that we know happened off-screen, in America—in our own Pennsylvania?

reviewText:
a lot of fun but also a mess, narratively. 
feels like an amalgam of Bong Joon Ho tropes.

- I love Robert Pattinson’s acting 
- Title card at 35 minutes in the runtime is so much fun
- The title (and in general, just having watched a lot of movies) tells you everything (I mean, the plot is not subtle at all)
- The evil characters are comedic and just present enough on screen to deserve their foils; Mark Ruffalo seems to be having a real good time, and Toni Collette manages to terrify as well as tickle
- Naomi Ackie!!! she played self-possessed, protective girlfriend so well
- The creepers are kinda cute, and I love this whole talking animals thing we have going on (I wonder if Bong Joon Ho plays a Bard, or maybe a Druid)

title: Munchausen, 2013 - ★★★
reviewText:
By nursing secret destructive attitudes, a wife can drive her husband, and a mother her children, into illness, accident, or even death. Or she may decide to keep the children from marrying—a deeply hidden form of evil that rarely comes to the surface of the mother's conscious mind. (A naïve old woman once said to me, while showing me a picture of her son, who was drowned when he was 27: "I prefer it this way; it's better than giving him away to another woman.")A strange passivity and paralysis of all feeling, or a deep insecurity that can lead almost to a sense of nullity, may sometimes be the result of an unconscious animus opinion. In the depths of the woman's being, the animus whispers: "You are hopeless. What's the use of trying? There is no point in doing anything. Life will never change for the better."The process of individuation: Animus, the man within
 M-L von Franz, excerpt from Man and His Symbols
